coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence detection of Cu2+ ions by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide composites
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yipeng
Li, Ruirui
Zhu, Zikang
Liu, Jun
Pan, Peng
Qi, Yangyang
Yang, Zhengchun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and ZnO (N-CQDs/ZnO) composites were prepared to improve the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) performance of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for detecting copper ions. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were prepared by a pyrolysis method using glucosamine as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source. In addition, N-CQDs/ZnO composites were prepared using zinc acetate in the same way, and they were characterized by the ECL method. The ECL performance of composite ZnO materials with paper-based modified electrodes improved, and the principle of signal enhancement of the N-CQDs/ZnO composite material was demonstrated. The paper electrode was modified with the composite material to detect copper ions via the quenching reaction. The results show that the sensitivity and detection limit (6.13 × 10−9 M) of the paper ECL sensor improved across a wide linear detection range (1.0 × 10−8–1.0 × 10−3 M). Therefore, N-CQDs/ZnO composites prepared by this method have potential applications in ECL signal amplification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
N-CQDs/ZnO
Electrochemiluminescence (ECL)
Paper electrode
Copper ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of copper ion detection by N-CQDs/ZnO composites
Sensing of copper ions by N-CQDs/ZnO composites on paper-based electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Tianjin Development Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship
National Natural Science Foundation of China

Natural Science Foundation of Tianjin City

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) particle size distribution of N-CQDs; and (d)–(f) TEM images of N-CQDs/ZnO at different magnifications.
UV–Vis spectra of (a) N-CQDs and (b) N-CQDs/ZnO (inset shows quantum dots under fluorescent light and at 365-nm UV light). Fluorescence spectra of (c) N-CQDs and (d) N-CQDs/ZnO.
(a) CV curves and (b) ECL intensity of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/ZnO. (c) CV curves and (d) ECL intensity of N-CQDs/ZnO after the addition of 0.1 mM Cu2+ ions.

(a) Relationship between ECL intensity and corresponding Cu2+ concentration. (b) Linear fits of the logarithms of Cu2+ concentrations to the corresponding ECL intensities. (c) Experiment on the selectivity of Cu2+ ions.
Comparison of proposed and previously reported Cu2+ detection methods.

head.ce:title: Electrochemiluminescence detection of Cu2+ ions by nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering, Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering
Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering, Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and ZnO (N-CQDs/ZnO) composites were prepared to improve the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) performance of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for detecting copper ions. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were prepared by a pyrolysis method using glucosamine as a carbon source and urea as a nitrogen source. In addition, N-CQDs/ZnO composites were prepared using zinc acetate in the same way, and they were characterized by the ECL method. The ECL performance of composite ZnO materials with paper-based modified electrodes improved, and the principle of signal enhancement of the N-CQDs/ZnO composite material was demonstrated. The paper electrode was modified with the composite material to detect copper ions via the quenching reaction. The results show that the sensitivity and detection limit (6.13 × 10−9 M) of the paper ECL sensor improved across a wide linear detection range (1.0 × 10−8–1.0 × 10−3 M). Therefore, N-CQDs/ZnO composites prepared by this method have potential applications in ECL signal amplification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of zero-dimensional carbonaceous material that have attracted significant attention in recent years owing to their wide range of potential applications including bioimaging [1], sensing [2], and drug delivery [3]. They can be produced using facile synthetic procedures and have low toxicity and good solubility [4]. Similar to traditional quantum dots [5], CQDs show good electrochemiluminescence (ECL) performance [6], and researchers have increasingly focused on methods to improve the electronic structure and optical properties of CQDs [7]. This is usually achieved by means of heteroatomic doping and surface functionalization. By doping with some nonmetallic elements such as nitrogen [8], sulfur, and phosphorus, chemical and fluorescence properties of CQDs can be enhanced; for example, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have improved photoelectric properties [9].

N-CQDs, an extension of carbon nanomaterials, have been explored in the field of ECL for the detection of metal ions and other substances. Niu et al. prepared multifunctional N-CQDs with unique and stable photoluminescence and electrochemiluminescence properties; these N-CQDs are widely usable in high-efficiency cell imaging, fiber staining, and specific sensitive detection of iron ions [9]. Zhou et al. developed a novel ECL immunosensor based on the quenching effect of aminated graphene (NH2-G)on N-CQDs [10]. They showed that the logarithm of ECL intensity and alpha fetoprotein concentration had a good linear relationship in a wide range under specified conditions, and the ECL sensor prepared therein exhibited good stability, selectivity, and reproducibility.

Carbon nanomaterial and metal oxide composites (CNMO) have a wide range of applications such as photocatalysts [11], gas sensors [12], and electrochemical sensors [13]. CNMOs not only retain the properties of carbon nanomaterials but also show an increased synergistic effect that greatly enhances the stability of metal oxides. Many studies have prepared CNMOs including N-CQDs/InNbO4 [14], PLA/WO3/N-CQDs [15], and N-CQD/Fe3O4 [16] and investigated their electrical and optical properties. For example, Nugraha et al. reported N-CQDs and WO3 composites that had high crystallinity, large surface area, and quenching photoluminescence intensity [17].

Zinc oxide (ZnO), a common metal oxide, has good catalytic activity, high specific surface area, and stabilization and electron migration abilities [18]; thus, it performs well as a modifier in chemically modified electrodes [19]. The production of ZnO-modified electrodes is advantageous because their raw materials are abundantly available and their fabrications costs are low [20]. ZnO has attracted much research interest owing to its wide band gap and good photoelectric performance. Behnaz et al. developed a novel electrochemical sensor based on a CQD, ZnO nanoflower, and poly (CTAB) composite for the simultaneous determination of paracetamol and ciprofloxacin [21]. Owing to the high conductivity and large surface area of CQDs and ZnO nanoflowers, and the synergistic effect of CQD/ZnO nanoflowers and poly (CTAB), multi-signal amplification is achieved, thereby improving the sensitivity and efficiency of the sensor system. Currently, numerous methods are available to combine quantum dots with ZnO, such as the solvothermal (ZnO/CQDs) [22], physical mixing (CQD/N-ZnO) [23], and hydrothermal (N-CQDs/Ni-ZnO) [24] methods. In the present study, an N-CQDs/ZnO composite was synthesized by pyrolysis through a simple and high-efficiency-one-step method.

Living organisms contain several essential elements, among which Cu2+ is the third-most abundant transition metal ion [25,26]. Copper ions are transferred from the soil to vegetables by fertilizers rich in copper oxide and then into plants and animals [27]. However, excessive Cu2+ consumption has been linked to diseases such as Alzheimer’s [28], Parkinson’s [29], and Wilson’s diseases [30]. Conversely, copper deficiency can lead to blood and nervous system disorders [31]. Therefore, an accurate analysis and determination of the Cu2+ content in samples is crucial for monitoring the environmental quality, drinking water, food safety, nutrition, and health status. Recently, many methods have been reported for Cu2+ detection, such as flame absorption atomic spectroscopy [32], inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry [33], sodium diethyldithiocarbamate trihydrate colorimetry [34], and electrochemiluminescence [35–37]. The present study focuses on electrochemiluminescence owing to its low signal-to-noise ratio and high luminescence purity [38], affording rapid detection and real-time feedback.

In this study, we investigated a composite of N-CQDs with ZnO, which was used as a co-reactant with KCl and K2S2O8 for the detection of Cu2+. The characterization and evaluation of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/ZnO allowed us to determine their physical properties and ECL activity.

Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and glucosamine were purchased from Beijing Solabao Technology Co. Urea, potassium chloride, and nickel chloride were purchased from Tianjin Windship Chemical Reagent Technology Co. Zinc acetate was purchased from Tianjin Kewei Co. Potassium persulfate was purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Supply and Marketing Co.; copper chloride, aluminum chloride, sodium chloride, iron chloride, and zinc chloride were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. All experiments were carried out with analytical-purity chemical reagents. Unless otherwise stated, deionized (DI) water obtained using the EASY ultrapure water system was used in this study.

The ECL measurements were performed on a model MPI-EII ECL analyzer (Xi'an Mindray Electronic Technology Co., ltd). Cyclic voltammetry(CV) was performed with a CHI 620E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., ltd).ECL detection was performed at room temperature.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) absorption spectra were measured using a UV-220 (Thermo Scientific) spectrophotometer and F-7000 (Hitachi) fluorescence spectrometer, respectively.Transmission electron microscopy(TEM) andhigh resolution transmission electron microscopy(HRTEM) images were obtained using a Talos F200X instrument (FEI Corporation of America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out with an ESCALAB250Xispectrometer(Thermo Scientific).Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy(FT-IR) was performed on a Thermo Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

A Cence-H2050R centrifuge (Hunan Xiangyi Centrifuge Co), an ME204 electronic balance (Mettler Toledo Instruments Co., ltd), and an MR Hei-TEC heated magnetic stirrer (Heidolph, Germany) were used for sample preparation.

In the pyrolysis method [39,40], 0.4 g of glucosamine and 1.2 g of urea were added to a porcelain crucible and heated on a heating table at 180 °C for 5 min (Fig. 1 a). A yellow–brown solid was obtained, which was then dissolved in 40 mL of DI water, forming a yellow–brown suspension. The suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to separate the agglomerated solid from the supernatant. The obtained N-CQDs supernatant was stored at 4 ℃. The quantum dot solution was purified and dialyzed with a Spectra/Por dialysis membrane (MWCO: 2000) in 4000 mL of DI water.

The N-CQDs/ZnO composite was prepared using the same method as described above: 0.4 g of glucosamine, 1.2 g of urea, and 0.4 g of zinc acetate were added to a porcelain crucible and heated on a heating table at 180 °C for 5 min (Fig. 1b). A yellow–brown solid was obtained, which was then dissolved in 40 mL of DI water, forming a yellow–brown suspension. The suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to separate the agglomerated solid from the supernatant. The obtained N-CQDs/ZnO supernatant was stored at 4 ℃. The composite mixture was purified and dialyzed using a Spectra/Por dialysis membrane (MWCO: 2000) in 4000 mL of DI water.

We have previously reported on paper-based ECL detection and fabrication of paper-based working electrode (PWE) [41]. The optimal detection conditions are determined based on the previous literature [42,43]. Owing to the same reaction mechanism, we chose a pH of 7.4 and a co-reactant concentration of 0.1 M K2S2O8.

To evaluate the ability of the composite to detect the ECL intensity, 20 μL of the N-CQDs/ZnO solution was dropped onto the surface of a PWE, washed with deionized water, and dried at room temperature. The obtained N-CQDs/ZnO/PWE was then fixed and connected to the ECL analysis equipment. The ECL measurements were performed in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M KCl as the electrolyte and 0.1 M K2S2O8 as a co-reactant. The scanning potential range was from − 2 to 0 V with a scan rate of 150 mV s−1 and a photomultiplier voltage of 900 V.

Fig. 2 a, b show the TEM and HRTEM images of the prepared N-CQDs. The TEM images show that the N-CQDs are relatively uniform in size (∼5 nm). Clearer streaks can be observed in the high-magnification microscope images, and the lattice spacing is estimated to be 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction plane of graphene. The particle size distribution (Fig. 2c) shows that the average particle size of the N-CQDs is ∼ 3 nm.

Fig. 2d–f show the TEM images of the prepared N-CQDs/ZnO at different magnifications. It can be observed that the ZnO is spherical with a large specific surface area (Fig. 2d, e). From Fig. 2f, we can see that ZnO surface has many deep and shallow layers as well as some pore-like structures. Accordingly, we believe that the composite is quite porous [19,44], which makes it easier for N-CQDs to attach and provide electron transfer channels [45]. Thus, the photoelectric properties of quantum dots and the ECL performance of the composite were improved [46].

Fig. 3 a, b show the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/ZnO solutions and the optical photographs recorded under room light (left) and under 365-nm UV light (right). The peak at 296 nm (Fig. 3a) belongs to the n–π* transition in the CO band and the π–π* transition in the CC band, indicating the formation of carbon-based quantum dots [47]. Fig. 3b shows a wider absorbance in the wavelength range of 270–380 nm compared to that shown in Fig. 3a, indicating an overall enhancement of the light absorption intensity. The ZnO composite emits brighter light under UV irradiation. Fig. 3c, d show the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/ZnO at different excitation wavelengths (340–440 nm), with an increase of 20 nm for each excitation wavelength. Fig. 3c shows that the emission peak of N-CQDs solution shifts from 410 to 520 nm when the excitation wavelength increases from 340 to 440 nm. The dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity on the excitation wavelength is a common phenomenon in carbon-based quantum dots. After forming a composite with ZnO, the material maintains the excitation-wavelength-dependent nature of N-CQDs.

To further investigate the elemental composition of N-CQDs/ZnO composites, XPS analysis was performed. As shown in Fig. S1, the diffraction peaks of four elements (C, N, O, and Zn) appear in the samples after compositing. After compositing with ZnO, the contents of both N and Zn were found to increase. Fig. S2 shows the split peak patterns of the high-resolution XPS spectra of the four diffraction peaks of the N-CQDs/ZnO composites. Fig. S2a shows the high-resolution XPS patterns of the characteristic peaks of C 1 s; the peaks at binding energies of 284.7, 286.2, and 288.6 eV are attributed to CC/CC, CO/CN, and CO/CN, respectively. The XPS signal of N 1 s at 399.2 eV (Fig. S2b) indicates the formation of pyridine N, which is different from the pyrrole N formed by the hydrothermal method. The higher content of N in the composites suggests that these will have superior luminescence [48]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the characteristic peak of O 1 s (Fig. S2c) has a binding energy of 531.6 eV. The two peaks observed in the binding energy spectrum of Zn 2p (Fig. S2d) are Zn 2p3/2 (1021.9 eV) and Zn 2p1/2 (1045 eV) with a peak spacing of 23.1 eV, which is consistent with the reported value for ZnO [49–51].

Fig. 4 a shows a comparison of the CV curves for the voltage interval between − 2 and 0 V. The N-CQDs/ZnO composite material has an electrochemical reduction peak at a voltage of approximately − 1.04 V. As shown in Fig. 4b, forming a composite with ZnO results in a significant enhancement of the ECL intensity. The reason for this phenomenon is that due to the negative excitation voltage interval, the N-CQDs/ZnO composite enhances transfer of electrons to the cathode, making electron transfer in the reduction process of the composite faster. This generates more excited states of quantum dots, resulting in increased emission and a stronger ECL signal. The composite had a good ECL stability under 10 consecutive cyclic potential scans (Fig. S3), with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.1 %. The ECL process of the N-CQDs/ZnO* and K2S2O8 co-reactant system can be expressed as Eqs. (1)–(4). In the first instance, N-CQDs/ZnO are reduced to N-CQDs/ZnO−, while the co-reactants of S2O8 are reduced to the oxidation state of SO4 −. Then, N-CQDs/ZnO− reacts with SO4 − to form the radical N-CQDs/ZnO*, which decays back from N-CQDs/ZnO and ECL emission is generated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of copper ion detection by N-CQDs/ZnO composites
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal detection conditions, a 40 μL PBS solution containing 0.1 mM Cu2+ ions, 0.1 M KCl, and 0.1 M K2S2O8 was added dropwise to the paper-based electrode modified with N-CQDs/ZnO composites; the cyclic voltametric and voltage-light intensity curves obtained are shown in Fig. 4c, d. In the N-CQDs/ZnO system, a negative potential is applied, and ZnO increases transfer of electrons to the cathode, and the ECL is enhanced. Upon the addition of Cu2+, the Cu2+ ions and ZnO complex block the electrode, hindering electron transport and leading to a significant burst in the ECL. This is due to the electron transfer of the radical N-CQDs/ZnO* after the addition of Cu2+ ions and the depletion of the N-CQDs/ZnO* radical, resulting in a significantly reduced ECL signal. Fig. S4 shows the principle diagram of ECL burst of Cu2+ ions in the N-CQDs/ZnO-K2S2O8 system.

The effect of the pH of the PBS solution and the concentration of the co-reactants on obtaining the optimal ECL signal was investigated. The ECL intensity of the PBS solution was measured over a pH range of 5–9. As shown in Fig. S5, the ECL intensity of N-CQDs/ZnO reaches a maximum at a pH of 7.4. Therefore, the optimal condition of pH = 7.4 was used throughout the study. The ECL intensity was measured for K2S2O8 concentrations between 0.05 and 0.2 M. As shown in Fig. S6, the maximum ECL intensity is obtained for N-CQDs/ZnO when the K2S2O8 concentration is 0.1 M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of copper ions by N-CQDs/ZnO composites on paper-based electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: We further investigated the ability of the paper-based electrodes modified with N-CQDs/ZnO composites to detect Cu2+. Fig. 5 a shows the trend comparison of the ECL intensity curves after adding different concentrations of Cu2+. The ECL signal decreases with increasing Cu2+ concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−8–1.0 × 10−3 M. Upon the addition of 0.01 μM Cu2+, the light intensity signal reached its maximum and was similar to that without the addition of Cu2+. Fig. 5b shows the linear fit curve between different concentrations of Cu2+ (X = lg c). Taking the logarithm of the concentration and the corresponding ECL intensity, the linear equation obtained was Y = 64.41–239.31X (R2 = 0.996) and the limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 6.13 × 10−9 M (S/N ratio of 3).

When calculating the LOD, we substituted the mean value of the blank samples (n = 10) into the Y value of the linear equation; the resulting X value was the corresponding value of LOD on the X-axis, which was finally transformed into a specific concentration value.

To confirm the specificity for Cu2+ ions on the paper-based sensor modified by the N-CQDs/ZnO composite, some common metal chlorides were explored. Fig. 5c shows the luminescence intensities obtained by adding PBS solutions containing the same concentrations (10 μM) of different metal chlorides (FeCl3, ZnCl2, NaCl, KCl, MnCl2, NiCl2, and AlCl3) on N-CQDs/ZnO/PWE under the same conditions. The ECL intensity significantly decreases when 10 μM CuCl2 is added but not when other metal chlorides are added. The light intensity slightly decreases when FeCl3 is added. Table 1 presents a comparison of the proposed Cu2+-detection method with previously reported ones. The results show that the proposed paper-based ECL sensor with N-CQDs/ZnO-modified electrodes afforded preliminary selectivity in the detection of Cu2+ ions.

In this study, we developed a simple environment-friendly and cost-effective direct heating method for the preparation of N-CQDs/ZnO composites. The N-CQDs/ZnO composites were compared with N-CQDs prepared by the same method and characterized by TEM, photoluminescence, and XPS analyses to demonstrate the successful synthesis of N-CQDs/ZnO composites. Subsequently, ECL tests were performed using paper-based electrodes modified by the composite material and the results were compared with those obtained with the material without ZnO. The composite material exhibited an excellent ECL performance with a significant improvement in luminescence intensity and stability, which may be due to the large specific surface area and high electron mobility of the ZnO-modified electrode, and the formation of more active sites in cathode discharge owing to the combination with quantum dots. After a series of optimizations, the optimal detection conditions were identified for the detection of Cu2+ ions. The system was found to exhibit a burst effect owing to the combination of Cu2+ ions and ZnO hindering electron transfer at the electrode, thereby weakening the ECL. The system showed some improvement in the linearity of the detection of the Cu2+ ions, and the paper-based sensor showed good selectivity for Cu2+ in the presence of other metal chlorides. In summary, the prepared N-CQDs/ZnO composites show good potential for signal amplification in ECL detection.

Yipeng Li: Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ruirui Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Zikang Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jun Liu: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Peng Pan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology. Yangyang Qi: Data curation. Zhengchun Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support by Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 18JCZDJC99800), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51502203), Tianjin Distinguished Professor Foundation of Young Researcher, and Tianjin Development Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108073.
